name: P-Mitrale
identifyBy: Notched or bifid P wave shape ('two humps') in lead II
Biphasic P wave in V1 with deep wide terminal negative component
P wave amplitude normal (<2.5mm) — it is broad, not tall
Most prominent in lead II and V1

measurements.pWaves: Broad, notched ≥120ms — bifid in II; biphasic in V1

management: P-mitrale = left atrial enlargement — treat the underlying cause
Common causes: mitral valve disease (stenosis, regurgitation), hypertension, cardiomyopathy, longstanding AF
AF risk: LAE is the strongest ECG predictor of AF — monitor and consider anticoagulation risk stratification
Echocardiogram: confirms LA size, mitral valve morphology and gradient
Mitral stenosis: percutaneous balloon mitral valvotomy or surgical repair/replacement
pearls: P-mitrale mnemonic: 'Massive P' = broad and wide (vs Pulmonary P = Pointed and peaked)
The inter-atrial delay causes the bifid P: right atrium activates (first hump), then slow conduction to left atrium (second hump)
V1 biphasic P: first positive deflection = right atrium, deep terminal negative = delayed LA activation
Longstanding mitral stenosis: chronic LAE → AF → loss of P waves
The degree of LA enlargement on ECG does not reliably predict LA dimension — echo is required for quantification
differentials: P-pulmonale (tall peaked narrow P vs broad notched)
Normal variant with slight P notching (duration <120ms)
Intraatrial conduction delay / Bachmann bundle block (similar but distinct mechanism)
